reasoning:
To resolve positively, Canada must experience more than ten times the baseline of 'Explosions/Remote violence' events in the 30 days preceding each resolution date. The baseline reference value (one plus the 30-day average over the last 360 days) was approximately 1.083 as of late October 2025. This equates to 1 recorded event over the previous 360 days. Thus, a positive resolution requires at least 11 such events to occur in a single 30-day window.

The ACLED 'Explosions/Remote violence' category predominantly covers highly destructive, non-accidental weapons deployed by an organized armed group or actor (e.g., IEDs, airstrikes, artillery, etc.). Historically, such incidents in Canada are exceptionally rare. A jump from 1 event per year to 11+ events in a single month would require an unprecedented nationwide terror campaign or intense armed conflict, neither of which has any precursor or credible indicator in recent times or early indications for Canada. With the 30-day window for the first date (Nov 2) already overwhelmingly in the past revealing no evidence of a dramatic upsurge, the probability is extremely low. Adhering to the limits of the forecasting rules and increasing uncertainty incrementally for progressively more distant horizons, my probabilities are confidently placed at the minimum reasonable figures allowed.
